critic_raw: The multi-spectral CNN for algal biomass prediction experiment has two methodological concerns. First, the ±2-day sampling tolerance introduces label noise proportional to bloom dynamics; highly variable lakes may have substantial biomass changes within this window. Second, the year-based temporal split means validation and test are each a single year; anomalous bloom years would produce unrepresentative performance estimates without year-level bloom characterization.
all_issues_raised: ±2-day sampling tolerance introduces label noise proportional to bloom dynamics; highly variable lakes may have substantial biomass changes within this window, biasing MAE estimates.

Year-based temporal split means validation and test are each a single year; anomalous bloom years (unusual weather) would produce unrepresentative performance estimates without year-level bloom characterization.

round_transcripts.critic: The multi-spectral CNN for algal biomass prediction experiment has two methodological concerns. First, the ±2-day sampling tolerance introduces label noise proportional to bloom dynamics; highly variable lakes may have substantial biomass changes within this window. Second, the year-based temporal split means validation and test are each a single year; anomalous bloom years would produce unrepresentative performance estimates without year-level bloom characterization.
round_transcripts.defender: On ±2-day label noise: the design explicitly excludes measurements flagged as delayed or non-standard, and uses HPLC or gravimetric analysis — rigorous protocols that minimize measurement error conditional on the sampling date. The ±2-day tolerance is a practical constraint of field sampling logistics; stricter windows would eliminate most paired observations. The label noise this introduces affects both the CNN and RGB baseline equally, so its effect on the relative comparison is symmetric. It would inflate absolute MAE estimates but not bias the model comparison. On year-based split: years 1-3 provide a 3-year training window, and test performance on year 5 is necessarily conditional on that year's conditions. Reporting per-lake test MAE (a secondary metric in the design) allows detection of anomalous lakes or years. The design should add a brief characterization of year-to-year biomass variability across the study period to contextualize whether year 5 is representative.

round_transcripts.critic: On label noise: the defense correctly notes that symmetric label noise affects both models equally, so the comparison is fair. This issue is resolved for the relative comparison, though absolute MAE interpretation remains affected. On year-based split anomaly: the defense agrees that year-level biomass characterization should be added to the design. Without it, year 5 test performance cannot be interpreted. This remains an open specification gap — not a fatal flaw, but a missing analysis that could reveal whether test-year conditions are representative.
round_transcripts.defender: Label noise issue resolved for comparative purposes. Year-level biomass characterization is a straightforward addition to the design — compute mean and variance of in-situ biomass by year across all lakes and report this alongside test results. This would allow readers to assess whether year 5 is anomalous. The design should include this contextual characterization in the results section.
